content.text: A capability gap analysis is a structured comparison between the AI capabilities an organization needs to deliver a use case and what it can realistically build internally, used to make an evidence-based build-vs-buy decision. Without this analysis, organizations either overbuild (spending 12 months building what a vendor already provides) or underbuy (contracting for commodity capabilities that constrain future differentiation).
What it is

A capability gap analysis for build-vs-buy decisions maps required capabilities (what the use case demands) against current internal capabilities (what the engineering team can build and maintain) and vendor capabilities (what is available on the market). The gap — the difference between required and internal — is then assessed against vendor solutions: can a vendor close the gap at acceptable cost and risk, or does the gap require internal development because no vendor solution fits?

The analysis produces a recommendation across three options: Build (develop the capability internally), Buy (procure a vendor solution), or Partner (co-develop or deeply integrate with a vendor that provides most but not all of the capability). Each option is evaluated on time-to-value, total cost of ownership, vendor lock-in risk, differentiation value, and data control requirements.
Why it matters

The AI capability landscape is bifurcated: some capabilities are genuinely differentiating (proprietary training data, unique workflow automation, domain-specific fine-tuning), while others are commodity (general-purpose LLM inference, standard RAG pipelines, generic tool-use scaffolding). Building commodity capabilities is one of the most expensive mistakes an AI engineering team can make — it consumes months of engineering time on problems that vendors have already solved at scale.

Conversely, buying a vendor solution for a genuinely differentiating capability creates lock-in: the vendor now owns the competitive advantage, and any price increase or policy change directly threatens the organization's differentiation. The capability gap analysis provides a principled framework for distinguishing commodity from differentiating capabilities before the build-vs-buy decision is made.
Benefits
Speed-to-value: buying commodity capabilities reduces time-to-production from months to weeks, allowing engineering capacity to focus on differentiation.
Strategic clarity: the gap analysis forces explicit articulation of which capabilities provide competitive advantage and should therefore be built internally.
Budget efficiency: avoiding the build-what-can-be-bought anti-pattern frees engineering budget for capabilities where internal development creates genuine value.
Risk reduction: the partner option provides a middle path for capabilities where no vendor solution is a perfect fit but full internal development carries excessive risk.
Defensible decisions: documented capability gap analysis provides an audit trail for procurement decisions, important for organizations subject to spend controls or technology governance.
Vendor negotiation leverage: a documented gap analysis that shows you evaluated alternatives gives procurement teams credibility when negotiating with vendors.
Trade-offs and limitations

Gap measurement subjectivity: estimating the size of a capability gap requires engineering judgment that may vary between team members. Use structured interviews and spike prototypes to validate estimates.

Vendor landscape volatility: the AI vendor market changes rapidly. A gap that required internal development 6 months ago may now be addressable by a vendor solution. The analysis needs regular refresh.
Hidden costs in buy scenarios: vendor pricing that looks cheap at evaluation time may have steep volume tiers at production scale. Model the total cost at 2x and 5x projected usage.

Build-bias in engineering teams: engineers tend to underestimate the complexity of building and maintaining capabilities, and overestimate the speed of internal development. Counteract this with realistic build estimates from engineers who have done similar work.

Buy-bias in procurement teams: procurement may underestimate integration complexity and overestimate vendor solution fitness. Always run a technical proof-of-concept before committing to a buy decision.
How it works

The analysis runs in three phases. Phase 1 (capability mapping): decompose the use case into atomic capabilities (e.g., 'multi-step tool orchestration', 'domain-specific entity recognition', 'real-time data grounding'). For each, rate the current internal capability on a 1-5 scale: 1 = no capability exists, 3 = partial capability requiring significant extension, 5 = fully capable with no gap.

Phase 2 (vendor survey): for each capability with a gap (score < 5), identify available vendor solutions and rate their fit against your requirements on the same 1-5 scale. Include cost, deployment model (cloud-hosted vs. self-hosted), compliance, and integration complexity in the fit assessment.
The differentiation test

For each capability with a vendor solution available (fit >= 3), apply the differentiation test: 'If a competitor used the same vendor for this capability, would our product still be differentiated?' If yes, it is likely a commodity capability and the Buy option is preferred. If no — if your differentiation depends on owning this capability — the Build option is preferred even when a vendor solution exists.
Weighting capability dimensions by strategic alignment

Not all capability gaps carry equal weight. A gap in a dimension that is central to your competitive differentiation deserves a higher weight multiplier than a gap in a commodity function. Before scoring, map each dimension to one of three strategic categories: differentiating (weight ×2.0), enabling (weight ×1.0), or hygiene (weight ×0.5). For example, if your agents must call proprietary pricing logic, custom orchestration code is differentiating and a build gap score of 4 becomes 8 after weighting—often enough to tip a borderline decision toward build.
Revisiting the gap map on a six-month cadence

Vendor capabilities evolve rapidly. A tool that scored 4 on agentic tool-calling quality six months ago may now score 8 after a major model update, flipping the build recommendation. Institutionalise a bi-annual review of your gap map: pull the latest vendor eval results, re-run the scoring harness, and diff the weighted totals against the previous run. If the delta exceeds 15%, escalate the result to the platform steering committee for re-decision. Store each snapshot in version control to preserve an audit trail of how the landscape changed.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Capability gap build vs buy framework: assessment, decision criteria, and recommendation path

content.text: The analysis maps required capabilities against internal and vendor capabilities, computing the gap for each. Capabilities are classified as commodity (high vendor fit, low differentiation) or strategic (low vendor fit or high differentiation). Commodity capabilities default to the Buy recommendation; strategic capabilities default to Build. Partner is recommended when vendor fit is moderate and full internal development carries excessive risk or timeline.
When to use it
New AI initiative planning: run the full gap analysis before committing to any technology stack for a new agentic AI product.
Capability refresh: re-run a targeted analysis when a new vendor solution enters the market for a capability currently being built internally.
Build cost overrun: when an internal build is exceeding its estimate by more than 50%, re-run the gap analysis to determine whether a buy or partner option is now more attractive.
Strategic planning: run the gap analysis annually as part of technology strategy to identify which capabilities are shifting from differentiating to commodity.
How to implement

Decompose the use case into atomic capabilities: list every distinct technical capability required to deliver the use case, at the level of granularity where build-vs-buy decisions can be made independently.

Rate internal capability for each: use a 1-5 scale with explicit rubric (1 = no existing code, 3 = working prototype requiring significant extension, 5 = production-ready). Get ratings from 2-3 engineers and average.

Survey the vendor market: for each capability with an internal score below 5, identify at least 2 vendor solutions. Rate each on fit (1-5), cost (model at expected production volume), and integration complexity.
Apply the differentiation test: for each capability where vendor fit >= 3, explicitly answer whether this capability is differentiating. Document the rationale.

Compute the recommendation: capabilities with vendor fit >= 3 and no differentiation → Buy. Capabilities with vendor fit < 3 or with differentiation → Build. Capabilities with vendor fit 3-4 and differentiation concerns → Partner.
Model the TCO: for each recommendation, model the total cost of ownership over 3 years including integration, maintenance, and expected usage growth.
Document and socialize: the gap analysis document should be reviewed by engineering leadership, security, procurement, and legal before final decisions are made.
Examples
Real-world example

A financial services firm was planning an internal AI assistant for compliance analysts. Their engineering team had proposed building everything from scratch: their own vector database, their own RAG pipeline, their own LLM gateway, and their own UI. The estimated timeline was 18 months. A capability gap analysis revealed that 7 of 9 required capabilities had high-fit vendor solutions and were not differentiating (standard RAG pipeline, vector database, LLM gateway, UI framework, observability, auth, and deployment). Only 2 capabilities were genuinely differentiating: proprietary financial regulation parsing and a custom citation verification workflow.

The analysis recommended buying 7 capabilities (estimated 6 weeks to integrate) and building 2 (estimated 4 months). Total time-to-production dropped from 18 months to 5 months. The engineering team's capacity was focused entirely on the capabilities that created competitive value, rather than rebuilding commodity infrastructure. The buy costs ($85K/year across 3 vendors) were a small fraction of the 13 months of engineering time saved.
Common mistakes
Confusing 'we could build this' with 'we should build this': technical feasibility does not imply build is the correct decision when vendor solutions are available.
Applying build-vs-buy at the product level rather than capability level: the correct unit of analysis is individual capabilities, not entire products.
Underestimating the maintenance cost of built capabilities: the build decision includes ongoing maintenance, security updates, and capability upgrades for the lifetime of the system.
Not running a proof-of-concept before committing to a buy decision: vendor claims of 'just works out of the box' frequently require significant integration work to fit the actual use case.
Assuming vendor solutions are static: vendor APIs change, pricing changes, and companies get acquired. The buy decision must include an exit plan.
Best practices
Run spike prototypes (1-2 day time-boxed experiments) before estimating internal build complexity — estimates without code are systematically optimistic.
Treat the buy-vs-build analysis as a living document: revisit it when vendor solutions enter the market or when internal build estimates prove inaccurate.
Model vendor cost at 2x and 5x expected usage to avoid bill shock as the system scales — many vendor pricing tiers have step functions that are punitive at scale.
Insist on a proof-of-concept for any buy decision: 30 days of integration work before contract signature is cheaper than 6 months of integration work after.
Document the differentiation rationale for every Build recommendation: if you cannot articulate why this capability creates competitive advantage, question whether Build is the right choice.
Performance and scalability

The capability gap analysis process itself typically takes 1-2 weeks for a moderately complex use case. The main scaling concern is vendor evaluation: evaluating more than 4-5 vendors per capability quickly becomes unwieldy. Focus vendor evaluation on the 2-3 best-fit solutions per capability and use request-for-information documents to reduce the evaluation depth for lower-priority capabilities.

For capabilities where the analysis is borderline (vendor fit = 3, differentiation = unclear), a time-boxed build spike (5 engineering days) combined with a vendor proof-of-concept (2-3 weeks) is the most reliable way to make the decision. The cost of a 3-week evaluation is trivial compared to the cost of committing to the wrong decision.

Standardize across vendors for fair comparison

Document outcomes of time-boxed build experiments

Technical vendor evaluation
Run capability benchmarks against vendor APIs

Link capability gap analysis to contract records
Comparison with alternatives
Decision approach
Strengths
Weaknesses
Capability gap analysis
Evidence-based, granular, defensible
Time-consuming; requires engineering input
Gut instinct / team preference
Fast; leverages experience
Systematically biased toward building; not defensible
Pure cost comparison
Objective on cost dimension
Ignores differentiation, risk, and strategic fit
Vendor-led evaluation
Low internal effort
Obvious conflict of interest; vendor will recommend buy
Market survey only (no internal assessment)
Good vendor coverage
Missing internal capability baseline; cannot compute gap
Security and reliability

The capability gap analysis document contains assessments of your organization's technical weaknesses and strategic gaps. Store it in access-controlled documentation with need-to-know access. Do not share it with vendors during negotiation — it reveals which of their weaknesses are acceptable to you, undermining negotiating leverage.

For reliability: the analysis is only as good as the internal capability ratings. Calibrate ratings across multiple engineers to reduce individual bias. If internal estimates of build time are consistently off by more than 50%, recalibrate by reviewing the actual vs. estimated time for the last 3-5 internal builds.
Key takeaways

Capability gap analysis decomposes a use case into atomic capabilities, assesses the gap between required and internal capability, evaluates vendor fit, and applies the differentiation test to determine Build, Buy, or Partner.
The differentiation test is the critical decision point: if a competitor using the same vendor solution would still be undifferentiated from you, the capability is commodity and should be bought.
Build decisions must include the full TCO: initial engineering time plus maintenance, security updates, and capability upgrades over the system lifetime.
Buy decisions must be validated by a proof-of-concept before contract signature — vendor fit claims require empirical verification.
Model vendor costs at 2x and 5x expected usage to avoid pricing surprises at scale.
The correct unit of analysis is individual capabilities, not entire products — the same initiative often has both Build and Buy recommendations for different capabilities.
Revisit the analysis when new vendor solutions enter the market or when internal build estimates prove significantly inaccurate.
